one_line_takeaway: If an attacker can write to an LLM's system prompt, three simple poisoning strategies -- automatable via the Auto-SPP helper-LLM framework -- persistently crater accuracy across all user queries (Gemini-2.5-flash: 93.2% -> 0.8% on MATH), hit reasoning models hardest, survive 500 conversation turns, and defeat the standard Explicit Reminder defense.
key_finding: SPP is highly effective, persistent, and evades the tested defense. On MATH, reasoning models collapse to near-zero in stateless API scenarios: Gemini-2.5-flash falls from a 93.2% baseline to 0.8% under brute-force poisoning in Explicit+API (a 99.1% relative drop), and GPT-5-mini from 91.4% to 3.0% (96.7% drop); reasoning-model drops exceed 96% while non-reasoning models degrade less (~50-70%), indicating reasoning models are MORE vulnerable because they more readily internalize the poisoned logic. Stateless API attacks are stronger than stateful interactive ones (accumulating benign history slightly dilutes poisoning), and MATH is hit harder than HumanEval (structured code offers some resistance). Persistence (RQ2): reasoning-model accuracy stays mostly below 15% across all 500 turns with nearly flat curves; non-reasoning models recover only modestly, more so under implicit prompts. Robustness (RQ3): user-side augmentation fails -- zero-shot CoT is the WORST mitigation, dropping accuracy to 1.8% (Adaptive ICL) / 2.4% (Adaptive CoT) because it makes the model reason using the only available (poisoned) patterns. Stealth (RQ4): the Explicit Reminder defense is completely ineffective, accuracy stays near-zero, because models treat the system prompt as the authoritative source over repeated benign user-level instructions. Cost (RQ5): brute-force is nearly free (1.9s / 0.7k tokens on MATH) while Adaptive CoT is far heavier (319.5s / 222.4k tokens).
core_contribution: The paper introduces and formally defines 'system prompt poisoning' (SPP), a new attack vector where an adversary who can modify an LLM's SYSTEM prompt (not the user prompt) persistently corrupts all subsequent outputs across an unbounded set of user inputs. This is contrasted formally with traditional user-prompt injection: injection finds one x_p such that f_M(s_t,x_p)!=f_M(s_t,x_t) for a single query, whereas SPP finds a poisoned system prompt s_p such that for ALL x_i in a set X, f_M(s_p,x_i)!=f_M(s_t,x_i). The authors propose three concrete poisoning strategies of their own: (1) brute-force poisoning (append explicit contradictory/biasing/hijacking instructions), (2) adaptive in-context poisoning (inject few-shot exemplars with wrong answers), and (3) adaptive chain-of-thought poisoning (inject exemplars with plausible-but-fallacious reasoning chains). They also build Auto-SPP, a framework that uses a helper LLM to automatically generate poisoned system prompts for an arbitrary task (Algorithm 1 in Appendix B). NOTE ON SCOPE: prompt injection (Perez & Ribeiro), indirect injection (Greshake et al.), the FATH test-time defense (Wang et al.), InjecAgent (Zhan et al.), PoisonedRAG (Zou et al.), and GCG jailbreaks (Zou et al.) are all CITED related work, NOT contributions of this paper. The paper's own novelty is the system-prompt (vs user-prompt) target and the persistence/breadth property.
threat_model: The paper's own stated threat model (Section 3 + Appendix A): the attacker's goal is to persistently generate malicious/wrong outputs for ALL user inputs by modifying the system prompt. The attacker CAN read and arbitrarily alter the system prompt, but has NO control over user prompts and NO knowledge of the specific LLM vendor, user input distribution, or whether ICL is applied. The core LLM itself is assumed to remain uncompromised (integrity intact). System-prompt access is assumed feasible via ACTIVE vectors -- exploiting software vulnerabilities in LLM-integrated apps (they cite CVE-2024-27564), compromising the software supply chain via vulnerable third-party libraries (e.g. a library Langchain uses to manage conversation history), or network-level man-in-the-middle interception -- or PASSIVE vectors -- distributing pre-poisoned apps, backdoored third-party libraries, or poisoned models/services through app stores, API marketplaces, or model/community hubs. This is a legitimate, well-specified threat model, not a forced security frame.
limitations: Only two task domains (MATH, HumanEval) and four models are evaluated, all on objective-answer tasks where 'wrong' is well-defined; open-ended generation is untested. The attack presupposes the attacker already has write access to the system prompt -- the paper motivates this with plausible vectors (CVEs, supply chain, MITM, malicious distribution) but does not itself demonstrate an end-to-end compromise, so feasibility rests on assumption. Only ONE defense (Explicit Reminder) is empirically tested; the stronger defenses the authors propose (System Prompt Integrity Monitoring via checksums/signatures, automated LLM auditing of exemplars, and instruction-hierarchy re-evaluation by providers) are discussed conceptually but not implemented or evaluated. RQ3/RQ4 are run on a single model (Gemini-2.5-flash) in limited scenarios. MATH uses a 500-sample subset (budget-limited), not the full set. Some reported model identifiers (e.g. 'GPT-5') and dates reflect the paper's stated setup.

datasets_used: MATH (Hendrycks et al. 2021), 500 randomly selected samples, solution accuracy
HumanEval (Chen et al. 2021), code generation, pass@1
quantitative_results: Gemini-2.5-flash Explicit+API MATH: 93.2% baseline -> 0.8% brute-force (99.1% drop), 2.4% Adaptive ICL (97.4% drop), 1.8% Adaptive CoT (98.1% drop)
GPT-5-mini Explicit+API MATH: 91.4% baseline -> 3.0% brute-force (96.7% drop)
Non-reasoning GPT-4o-mini Explicit+API MATH: 76.4% baseline -> 37.4% brute-force (51.0% drop)
Non-reasoning Gemini-2.5-flash (no-thinking) Explicit+API MATH: 89.6% -> 25.2% brute-force (71.9% drop)
Reasoning-model drops on MATH stateless API exceed 96%; non-reasoning drops ~50-70%
RQ2 persistence: reasoning-model accuracy stays mostly below 15% across 100/300/500 turns with near-flat curves
RQ3: under zero-shot CoT user augmentation, accuracy drops to 1.8% (Adaptive ICL) and 2.4% (Adaptive CoT)
RQ4: Explicit Reminder defense leaves accuracy near-zero across all three strategies (no meaningful protection)
RQ5 cost on MATH: brute-force 1.9s / 0.7k tokens; Adaptive ICL 268.4s / 123.6k tokens; Adaptive CoT 319.5s / 222.4k tokens (HumanEval: 2.2/2.2, 281.4/... , 340.4s and 1.3k/160.1k/246.9k tokens)
baselines_compared: 'No poisoning' benign-system-prompt baseline (per-configuration reference for the percentage-drop figures)
Explicit Reminder black-box prompt-injection defense (Yi et al. 2025) -- prepends benign system prompt to each user query; found completely ineffective
User-side prompt augmentation as implicit user defenses: two-shot ICL, zero-shot CoT, two-shot CoT -- all found ineffective
